title: Top Questions to Ask Your Digital Marketing Company
content:
Top Questions to Ask Your Digital Marketing Agency - We're here to help businesses understand the challenges in hiring a firm for their digital marketing. Being in the business professionally for over 20 years ongoing, it's amazing how - after all this time - it's still clear there are a ton of charlatans in the space. In fact, I'd argue - this is getting to be an even larger problem. The proliferation of simple tools that will visually do 90% of what a client needs, but requiring minimum skills - while at the same time the important parts of the process have (in some cases) gotten much more complex and complicated (an example is Google Analytics 4 and/or Google Tag Manager) coupled with the hustler economy where anyone can try to "outsource it" to someone that knows what they're doing - and it's a recipe for disaster.

So it's important to be cautious while searching for a digital marketing company.

Top Questions to Ask Your Digital Marketing Company

So what are you to ask your digital marketing company? Well look no further than the list below - we help you sort through the mess and provide you with a simple framework to work from.

Before we get to the list however, a quick step back...

What DO YOU Want to Accomplish?

Having worked with clients of all sizes over the last 20 years, I see this as a massive problem with companies and organizations of all sizes. No one has any idea what they want. Now, obviously a part of that is understanding what's realistic - what the market looks like - all things a competent digital marketing agency can assist with - but before you even talk with a marketing agency I highly recommend you think about where you'd like your company to be in 6 months - 1 year - and 5 years. The biggest problem I deal with time and time again - is that clients grow too quickly and although their marketing is on point and the deals are flowing in - they simply can't handle the additional work... they can't handle the additional sales, the additional administrative steps or have the team in the background to handle fulfillment.

Alright with that out of the way let's get to the meat and potatoes of this here post shall we...

Top Questions to Ask Your Digital Marketing Company

1) What services do you specialize in?

You want to understand if the agency's core strengths align with their specific needs, whether that's SEO, PPC, social media marketing, content marketing, email marketing, or another area. One important thing to understand is that in digital - everything overlaps. The way you build your website plays a HUGE role in your ability to rank via Search Engine Optimization. Keeping things nicely organized as a potential client, means your digital advertising company can do more, more quickly, and less expensive.

2) Can you show me examples of your past work or case studies?

Seeing tangible results achieved for other clients helps them gauge the agency's effectiveness and expertise in their industry or similar campaigns. This is hands down one of the most important top questions to ask your digital marketing company... why? Because the proof is in the pudding. Watch carefully for any kind of song and dance... did they actually develop the website? Did they design it in house? Did they do the SEO? How? What were the results? Similarly here - I'd recommend asking for a reference related to whatever case-study they provide - to ensure the #'s match with reality.

3) How do you measure success and report on campaigns?

As a client you are interested in how your prospective digital marketing agency tracks progress, the metrics used to measure success, and the frequency and detail of reporting they can expect. Does this align with your own business? Are they flexible? Does the information they provide help and provide value?

4) Who will be working on our account?

Understanding who in the agency will be directly involved with your project, their experience, and how communication will be handled is important. Knowing this ahead of time to the best of the companies ability is helpful and comforting. Often times sales persons often aren't involved in fulfillment (they can't). One frustration we've seen time and again is U.S. sales forces handing off support and ongoing activities to overseas teams that frustrate cleints to no end - but they're stuck in multi-year 6-figure agreements.

5) How do you stay up-to-date with industry changes and trends?

The digital marketing landscape is constantly evolving, so clients want to ensure their chosen agency keeps pace with the latest developments.

6) What is your strategy for our specific needs/goals?

Clients look for a tailored approach that shows the agency has not only understood their unique challenges but also has a strategic plan to address them.

7) How do you handle pricing and contracts?

Transparency about costs, what's included in the agency's fees, contract terms, and any other financial commitments is crucial for clients.

8) Do you offer any kind of guarantee?

A guarantee in today's day and age is fairly simple for a company to setup and uphold. If a company isn't willing to put their own money where their mouth is - odds are it's not going to be a great company. But watch out for companies that create less than transparent guarantees and offers that stick you with the bill - even if the company doesn't perform.

9) How do you ensure our brand's voice is accurately represented in your marketing efforts?

Clients want to make sure the agency can authentically convey their brand's identity and values in their marketing campaigns.

10) What makes your agency different from others?

Clients are looking for what sets the agency apart, whether it's their approach, team expertise, innovative solutions, or something else.
seo_title: Top Questions to Ask a Digital Marketing Agency: A Guide
seo_description: Digital Marketing: Get the answers you need! Ask Olympia Marketing the top questions to make smart choices for your business.
focus_keyword: Top Questions to Ask Your Digital Marketing Agency

title: 2024 Google Local Service Ads Ultimate Guide - What Are They And How Do You Optimize Them?
content:
In the rapidly evolving digital landscape, as a small businesses you face a constant challenge of standing out and connecting with your ideal customers. We've tested a LOT of services over the years - in virtually all industries. Knowing what's out there and what works is half the battle. From websites to seo to adwords to lead purchasing programs to mailers to cold calling to email to... well you get the point. There's an infinite number of ways to spend your businesses money. But what works? And how do you as a small business owner laser focus your time, attention and money on something that's going to provide results as quickly as possible? That's where we come in - and that's where Google's Local Services Ads also comes in.

Enter Google Local Service Ads (LSA) – a game-changer for businesses aiming to capture the attention of local customers ready to convert. Zach Katkin, a seasoned digital marketing expert from Olympia Marketing, brings over two decades of experience to the table, offering invaluable insights into the power of LSAs. From the sunny shores of Estero, Florida, Zach and his team at Olympia Marketing have mastered the art of leveraging LSAs to benefit a wide array of clients locally AND nationwide, particularly those in home services and real estate.

In this blog post, we delve into Zach's expert analysis on why Google Local Service Ads are not just another advertising tool but a critical component of a successful digital marketing strategy. Whether you're a plumber, electrician, realtor, or lawyer, understanding the nuances of LSAs could mean the difference between a lead that fizzles out and one that converts into a loyal customer. Join us as we explore how Olympia Marketing navigates the complexities of Google's qualified leads, ensuring their clients not only get noticed but dominate in their respective industries.

Google Local Service Ads Frequently Asked Questions

  • Frequently Asked Questions
  • How do I set up a Google Business Profile Account?
  • What’s crucial when verifying my profile?
  • How important are descriptions and categories in my listing?
  • Can reviews really impact my business listing?
  • What’s the deal with Google Posts?
  • Why should I monitor insights on my Google Business Profile?
  • How does optimizing my listing improve SEO?

Ever wondered why some brands seem to pop up everywhere online, while others stay hidden in the digital shadows? The secret sauce often lies in how well they've set up and optimized their Google ads. It's not just about throwing your name into the digital ring; it’s about making sure you stand out in a crowd of competitors clamoring for attention. In this bustling online marketplace, setting up your Google listing ads correctly can be the difference between being a needle in a haystack and the magnet that draws the needle out. Let's dive into how you can make your business shine brighter than Times Square on New Year's Eve by mastering the art of setting up and optimizing your Google listing ads.

Understanding Google Local Services Ads:

Check out the YouTube video we created below about Google Local Service Ads and what you need to be doing to optimize your own ads account.

What are Google Local Service Ads?

Google's LSA (or Google Local Services Ads) originally debuted in 2015 - so nearly 10 years ago now! But, it was only in 2023 that they finally expanded beyond a few select categories (notably the original tests of the product were in the HVAC and Air Conditioning industries). As of March of 2023 the service now has expanded to more than 70 different business categories in a number of major sections.

So the question is what are Google Local Service Ads and what do they mean for your business?

Well LSA ads are simply ads for companies that have been vetted by Google, that meet certain criteria and are served up in particular places. Additionally, unlike conventional Google Ads (say via AdWords) you pre-set a weekly budget and you're only charged when you receive qualified leads.

In a nutshell Google LSA Ads are DONE FOR YOU ADS, based in large part on your GOOGLE MY BUSINESS - for select industries - where you ONLY PAY for QUALIFIED LEADS.

No major tweaking, no trying to figure out keywords, no monitoring budgets and a 1000 different settings. If you're a business in select areas and disciplines, you can get a LOT more business immediately by using these ads to supplement your current marketing stack.

Are Google LSA Worth it For Your Small Business?

I've been thoroughly testing this tool for multiple businesses over the last year and I have to say that Google LSA ads are ABSOLUTELY a game changer for many industries and I highly recommend setting them up. In particular I've seen them do extremely well in the home services industries as well as for realtors locally - who REALLY struggle often-times with marketing - and only had massive (and expensive) services like Zillow and others to buy leads from.

Does it replace AdWords? Not at all. And in some ways it might even be a bit trickier than AdWords to compete on. But, I would now recommend that IMMEDIATELY following the setup of a businesses website - that all 3 happen at the same time:

  1. Google My Business Setup & Review Campaigns
  2. Google AdWords
  3. Google LSA Ads

Initial Key Takeaways on Google Local Service Ads

  • They're EXTREMELY Effective
  • Super Simple to Setup (Although Requires time to be Approved)
  • Setting up a Google Business Profile effectively starts with creating an account and verifying your profile, ensuring your business can be found by potential customers.
  • It's crucial to provide complete and accurate data entry, including contact information, to make it easier for customers to reach out to you.
  • Crafting compelling descriptions and choosing the right categories are key steps in making your listing appealing and relevant to your target audience.
  • Optimizing your profile with detailed descriptions, accurate categories, and precise location information can significantly improve your visibility in search results.
  • Leveraging reviews, attributes, and real images enhances trust and engagement with potential customers, showcasing the authenticity of your business.
  • Regularly updating your Google Posts and product catalogs, along with monitoring insights and customer interactions, are essential practices for keeping your listing engaging and informative.
  • Competition is Stiff in Some Categories - Being the Best Business You Can Be (IE more reviews) is the main way to get ahead)

Setting Up Your Google Business Profile Account

Getting Started

To kick off your journey in setting up a Google Business Profile, head over to Google My Business. Here, you'll find the "Manage now" button. Clicking this is your first step into the world of online business visibility.

Once inside, it's crucial to use a consistent business name and address that matches what you have listed elsewhere online. This consistency helps customers find you more easily and improves your credibility on the platform.

Choosing Categories

Selecting the right business category is like choosing where to set up shop in a large mall. It tells customers what you offer at a glance. Make sure it’s relevant and specific to what services or products you provide.

This choice can significantly impact how well potential customers can discover your service through Google searches. Think about what terms people might use when looking for businesses like yours and select accordingly.

Importance of Consistency

Consistency isn't just about using the same logo across platforms (though that's important too). It means ensuring every piece of information about your business matches everywhere it appears online.

Mismatched details can confuse potential customers and hurt your search rankings. So double-check addresses, phone numbers, and even spellings before finalizing them on your Google Business Profile.

Setting up an optimized Google listing involves careful attention to detail from start to finish. By following these steps—starting with navigating to Google My Business, ensuring consistency in your business information, and selecting an appropriate category—you lay down strong foundations for online success.

Remember: The basics go a long way in making sure that both search engines and humans understand who you are, what services or products you offer, and how they can reach out or visit you. Business owners should view their Google profile as an ongoing project rather than a one-time setup; always look for opportunities to update information or improve listings based on customer feedback or changes in service offerings.

Accessing and Verifying Your Profile

Verification Methods

After setting up your Google Business Profile account, the next crucial step is verifying your profile. This ensures that only you or someone you authorize can manage your business information on Google.

Google offers three methods for verification: postcard, phone, or email. The method available to you depends on the type of business and its location. For most businesses, verification by postcard is the standard method. Google will send a postcard to your business address with a unique verification code.

If eligible, phone and email options provide a quicker way to verify. You'll receive either a call or an email containing the verification code. It's essential to choose the method that works best for you to ensure timely completion of this process.

Entering Verification Code

Once you receive your verification code, it's time to enter it into your Google Business Profile. Accuracy here is key; make sure every number or letter matches exactly what was provided by Google.

Entering this code correctly verifies that you have rightful access to manage the business listing associated with that address or contact information. If entered incorrectly, it might delay the process as you may need to request another code and wait additional days for its arrival.

The entire verification process could take a few days from start to finish. Patience is vital during this period as rushing through steps could lead to errors in entering information or choosing an incorrect verification method for your specific situation.

  • To summarize:
  • Choose between postcard, phone, or email based on eligibility.
  • Enter the received verification code accurately.
  • Allow several days for completion of the verification process.

These steps are not just formalities but integral parts of ensuring that your listing's integrity remains intact—directly influencing how users view and interact with your page online.

Complete Data Entry and Contact Information Management

Accurate Fields

Filling in all fields on your Google listing ads is crucial. It ensures customers get the right information about your company. This includes hours of operation and special holidays. Every detail matters.

Accuracy cannot be overstressed. A mistake can mislead a customer, affecting their experience negatively. Always double-check what you enter, especially for critical info like operating hours.

Prompt Updates

When changes happen within your business, updating your contact information should be top priority. Whether it's a new phone number or an address change, keep it current to maintain control over how customers reach you.

Prompt updates prevent confusion and ensure that potential clients can always connect with you easily. Remember to add any new services or products as well to keep your listing fresh and appealing.

Error Checking

Typos are easy to make but hard to spot once they're part of your Google listing ads. Pay extra attention to phone numbers and URLs since these are direct lines for customers to call or visit online.

A single digit off in a phone number or a misplaced letter in a URL can lead to missed opportunities for engagement and sales. Double-checking these details safeguards against such pitfalls.

  • Pros:
  • Ensures accuracy
  • Enhances customer trust
  • Prevents lost opportunities
  • Cons:
  • Time-consuming
  • Requires regular monitoring
  1. Review all entered data for accuracy.
  2. Schedule regular checks for updates needed.
  3. Correct typos immediately upon discovery.

Crafting Compelling Descriptions and Choosing Categories

Writing Descriptions

Creating a concise, keyword-rich description of your business is crucial. This description helps customers understand what you offer. It should be easy to read and straight to the point.

Your first step is to list down the services or products you provide. Then, incorporate keywords that potential customers might use when searching for these services online. Remember, clarity is key. Avoid industry jargon that might confuse people who are not familiar with it.

For example, if you run a bakery specializing in gluten-free products, mention "gluten-free bakery" early in your description. Also include terms like "freshly baked," "all-natural ingredients," and specific items like "gluten-free bread" or "vegan pastries."

Choosing Categories

Selecting the right primary category and secondary categories for your listing can significantly impact its visibility.

The primary category should closely match what your business is most known for. If you're unsure which category fits best, look at competitors with similar offerings to see their choices.

Secondary categories give more context about your services but choose wisely; too many can dilute the focus of your listing.

Here's how to pick effective categories:

  • Review all available options on Google.
  • Think about what words customers would use to find a business like yours.
  • Select a primary category that most accurately describes your main service.
  • Use secondary categories for additional services you offer but aren't as prominent.

Optimizing Descriptions, Categories, and Location

Local Keywords

Incorporating local keywords into your Google listing ads is crucial. These are the words or phrases that people in your area use when searching for services like yours. By using them, you make it easier for potential customers to find you.

Think about what someone might type into a search bar. If you're a bakery in Seattle, they might search "Seattle bakery" or "cakes near me." Including these local keywords in your descriptions can boost your visibility in local search results.

Precise Location

Ensuring that your location details are precise is another key step. This means double-checking that your address is correct and easily locatable on Google Maps. A clear location helps customers find you without hassle.

It's not just about having the right address listed; it’s also making sure that any directions provided lead directly to your doorsteps. This boosts customer satisfaction and can enhance local ranking significantly.

Regular Reviews

Regularly reviewing and updating categories related to your business ensures relevance over time. Markets evolve, as do the ways people search for things online. Staying updated with these changes keeps you competitive.

For example, if new services are added or if there's a shift in how people refer to what you offer, adjusting categories accordingly can help maintain or improve visibility in searches.

Optimizing Your Google Local Service Ads - Leveraging Reviews, Attributes, and Real Images

Positive Feedback
Encouraging satisfied customers to leave positive reviews is crucial. These reviews boost your business's credibility. They show new customers that your products or services are reliable.

After optimizing descriptions and categories, focus on gathering customer feedback. Ask customers directly through emails or social media. Provide links to make leaving a review easy for them. Remember, more positive feedback means higher trust from potential customers.

Handling Reviews

Responding professionally to all reviews is essential. It shows you value customer feedback. Address negative comments with care and offer solutions.

A professional response can turn a negative review into a positive experience for others reading it. This approach demonstrates excellent customer service skills which can attract more clients.

Quality Images

Upload high-quality images of your business offerings. Photos should be clear and attractive.

Images play a key role in attracting customers online since they provide a visual representation of what you're selling or the service you're providing.

  • Cover photo: Choose an image that represents your brand well.
  • Product photos: Showcase what you sell with clear, well-lit pictures.

Using real images instead of stock photos adds authenticity to your listing.

Unique Attributes

Highlight unique attributes of your products or services in the images uploaded.

Attributes could include special features not found with competitors or unique offers available only at your business.

Listing these attributes helps set apart from others in the market.

Utilizing Google Posts and Product Catalogs for Engagement

Google Posts

Google Posts provide a unique way to communicate directly with your audience through your Google listing. They let you share timely updates, special offers, and events. Think of it as a blend between social media and search engine visibility.

By crafting engaging posts, you can catch the attention of potential customers who are conducting local searches. This is crucial because these users are often ready to make a decision or purchase. For example, announcing an upcoming sale or showcasing a new product can entice them to visit your store or website.

Remember, visuals play a key role here. A captivating image can make someone stop scrolling and read your post. Use high-quality photos that reflect the message you want to convey.

Product Catalogs

Adding products or services to your catalog is another effective strategy for leveraging your Google listing for more engagement. Detailed descriptions and accurate prices help customers understand what you offer without having to leave their search results page.

This direct approach simplifies the shopping experience for users conducting local searches on Google. If they find what they're looking for in your catalog, they might decide right then to do business with you.

To maximize impact:

  • Ensure all product images are clear and professionally taken.
  • Write concise but detailed descriptions.
  • Keep pricing up-to-date.

Both methods—using Google Posts and updating Product Catalogs—serve as powerful tools in enhancing online presence and driving customer engagement through search results pages.

Monitoring Insights and Customer Interactions

Trend Analysis

Regularly checking your dashboard for insights can reveal much about how prospective customers interact with your Google listing ads. Look out for trends in searches, views, and actions on your listing. This data is crucial because it tells you what's working and what's not.

For instance, if you notice an increase in views after posting a new product catalog, that’s a sign such content resonates with your audience. On the flip side, if certain posts see less engagement or actions (like clicks), it might be time to adjust your strategy.

Customer Engagement

Engaging promptly with customer questions in the Q&A section of your Google listing is key to maintaining good relations. It shows prospective customers that you value their inquiries and are responsive to their needs.

Remember, every interaction is an opportunity to convert interested users into loyal customers. Keeping this section updated and clear also helps reduce repetitive queries by providing answers upfront for common questions.

Optimization Timing

Analyzing peak times for customer interactions can significantly optimize your post timings on Google listings. By identifying when potential customers are most active, you can schedule posts during these windows to maximize visibility and engagement.

Here's how:

  1. Review insights from previous weeks or months.
  2. Spot patterns indicating when views or interactions spike.
  3. Schedule future posts around these peak times.

This approach ensures that more eyes land on what you have to offer at just the right moment.

Strategies for Enhancing SEO with Google My Business Features

Fresh Content Updates

Updating your profile consistently is key. Make sure all information is current. This includes hours, services, and contact details. Regular updates signal to Google that your business is active.

Adding new photos or posts weekly can boost engagement. It shows customers and search engines alike that you're involved in your business's online presence.

Strategic Keywords

Use keywords wisely in your profile. They should fit naturally into descriptions and posts without overstuffing. This helps improve local SEO significantly.

Identify the most relevant keywords for your local business first. Then, incorporate them throughout your profile thoughtfully.

Link Backs

Linking back to specific pages on your website from your listing drives traffic effectively. Use this feature to highlight special offers or important information about your services.

This not only boosts direct visits but also enhances overall SEO performance by creating valuable backlinks to your site.

Closing Thoughts

Setting up and optimizing your Google listing ads isn't just ticking boxes; it's about weaving your business's digital footprint into the fabric of the internet. You've walked through creating a robust Google Business Profile, from nailing the basics to diving deep into SEO strategies. This journey ensures your business shines brighter in the crowded online marketplace. Remember, it's not a one-and-done deal. The digital world evolves, and so should your strategies. Keep tweaking, testing, and turning feedback into fuel for growth. Your efforts here lay the groundwork for not just surviving but thriving in the digital ecosystem.

Now, take this knowledge and run with it. Dive back into your profile, apply these insights, and watch as your visibility skyrockets. And hey, if you hit a bump or uncover a new trick, share the wealth. Let's keep this conversation going and build a community of savvy businesses dominating the digital scene together.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I set up a Google Business Profile Account?

First off, head to the Google Business Profile website and click "Manage now." You'll need a Google account for this. Just follow the on-screen instructions—it's like assembling a piece of IKEA furniture, but easier!

What’s crucial when verifying my profile?

Think of verification as your golden ticket. Without it, you can't fully manage your profile. Typically, Google sends a postcard with a code to your business address. Once you receive it, enter the code in your profile setup page. It's straightforward but essential.

How important are descriptions and categories in my listing?

Imagine walking into a party and needing to introduce yourself; that's what descriptions and categories do for your business online. They tell potential customers who you are and what you're about at first glance—so make them count!

Can reviews really impact my business listing?

Absolutely! Think of reviews as word-of-mouth in digital form. Positive feedback acts like little cheerleaders for your business, encouraging others to check out what you offer.

What’s the deal with Google Posts?

Google Posts are like mini-advertisements or social updates that pop up directly in search results—a free shoutout from Google itself! Use them to promote events, offers, or new products directly where potential customers can see them.

Why should I monitor insights on my Google Business Profile?

Monitoring insights is akin to being handed a map in unfamiliar territory; it shows how customers find and interact with your listing—valuable intel for tweaking strategies towards better visibility and engagement.

How does optimizing my listing improve SEO?

Optimizing isn’t just sprinkling magic SEO dust; it involves sharpening every aspect of your listing—from accurate info down to engaging images—to ensure both people and search engines love what they see.

title: Google AdWords (PPC) Vs. Google Local Ads
content:
In today's exploration, we're diving into the dynamic world of digital advertising, focusing on two titans of the local service arena: Local Service Ads (LSA) versus Google Search Ads. Both wield considerable power in amplifying visibility and generating leads, but the question remains: which one reigns supreme? Join us as we delve into the nuances of each, armed with real-world examples to illuminate your strategic path.

LSA: The Local Vanguard

Local Service Ads represent Google's innovative approach to bridging the gap between service providers and local customers. Picture LSA as your community's digital billboard, emerging exclusively for users with a strong intent to engage within your vicinity. The highlight? Your investment is lead-focused, not click-dependent.

When LSA Outshines:

  • For Service-Oriented Local Enterprises: Whether you're a plumber, a locksmith, or an HVAC specialist, LSAs are your best ally. Imagine being a locksmith in Tulsa; your ad surfaces precisely when a local searches for "locksmith near me."
  • Elevated Trust Quotient: LSAs come with a vetting process and often a Google Guarantee, significantly boosting user confidence in your service.
  • Cost Efficiency for Small Ventures: The pay-per-lead model presents a more economical option for businesses where financial prudence is paramount.

Potential LSA Limitations:

  • Non-Service or Non-Local Entities: LSAs won't suit e-commerce platforms or businesses lacking a local presence.
  • Wider Audience Ambitions: LSAs may restrict your reach if your target is not strictly local.
  • Scaling Challenges: With LSAs, your growth is somewhat fixed; expanding beyond a certain point requires more than just budget adjustments.

Google Search Ads: The Universal Reach Maestro

Google Search Ads stand as the backbone of online advertising, unmatched in their reach and precision. They empower you to target a global audience, fine-tuning your campaign with specific keywords, bids, and creative inputs.

Google Search Ads Excel When:

  • Broad Audience Targeting: Ideal for entities not limited to local services, like digital course providers or e-commerce behemoths.
  • Tailored Campaigns: Leverage advanced targeting to sculpt your campaign around particular keywords, interests, and behaviors.
  • Boosting Brand Visibility: Launching a new product or elevating brand awareness? Search Ads can catapult your brand into the spotlight.

When Google Search Ads May Exceed Needs:

  • Budgetary Constraints: For smaller, local businesses, the competition for popular keywords might be financially daunting.
  • Immediate Trust Imperative: Unlike LSAs, Search Ads lack the instant credibility of a Google Guarantee.

Aligning Strategy with Ambitions

The choice between LSA and Google Search Ads hinges on a deep understanding of your business framework, target demographic, and overarching objectives. Are you courting local clientele or casting a wider, possibly global net? Tailor your advertising approach accordingly, and you're on the path to triumph.

Should you seek expert guidance on navigating Google Ads, consider partnering with our team. We proudly offer an LSA add-on, ensuring comprehensive coverage for your advertising needs. Let's elevate your digital presence together!

title: Google (and Others) Have NO IDEA What They're Doing
content:
I think there's this idea out there that these massive multi-national conglomerates, particularly in the tech space - attract the best and the brightest - and that - despite their size, they're masters at everything and anything.

As someone that interacts with many of these companies on a virtually daily basis - nothing could be further from the truth.

One quick example - before I get to the meat of this post - I was setting up a new account the other day and doing my normal stack of AdWords, Google Tag Manager, Analytics 4, Webmaster Tools (Search Console)... etc. etc. I've literally done this 100 times in the last 3 months with new clients.

In this ONE particular case though - I was surprised to find two errors I had never encountered before along with a bug. The bug is pretty major - as it basically makes it impossible to setup conversion tracking for websites utilizing adwords when you've linked both your adwords to your Google Analytics 4. After some major digging (as Google's support is almost ALWAYS worse than useless) - I was able to disconnect the link, fix the problem, and relink. The bug has been known about for more than a year. The actual services and "customer support" teams involved proved no help what-so-ever.

Another scenario - a client has had Google Local Services ads setup for quite some time. I recently took over management of the account - as it's not lead to much performance. Part of the question is - well is the market just slow? Or is there something going on.

In doing a deep dive - it turns out Google never connected the clients' Google My Business profile to their advertising account. Not to mention that Google Local Service Ads - although fantastic have a VERY strange interface (looks like it was built 15 years ago and is SUPER slow and has major issues with handling multiple accounts or account managers).

In another scenario I spoke to a Google rep a few weeks ago for another issue - and keep in mind here I'm DIRECTLY personally responsible for getting tens of thousands of businesses on Google's advertising services over the years and what is probably a $billion in total advertising. Even personally, for my businesses I've easily spent a half million or more over the years (right now ONE client is going to spend over $1million on AdWords this year alone). With all of this... I have no special support for all but the most extreme issues (I do have the head of AdWords large accounts contact information - which I'll come back to in a second). So I go the normal route and get a support rep that is COMPLETELY unintelligible and not helpful. Hours and hours wasted - just to be frustrated by this multi-cultural nightmare we find ourselves in.

Something similar happened with a few major services for websites and hosting - namely wix and squarespace. I'm a big fan of squarespace actually (I recommend it for any business owners or individuals looking to build their own websites), but there are just certain elements of the way it's structured that make no sense - and bugs and problems with the tools that have literally persisted for years and years and years.

Ironically, I write this as I'm sitting in a Panera. Even though this is a relatively small town - with high local trust, and wealth - they've decided to first install machines to order (which work half the time and their interfaces are slow and clunky the other half). At the same time they have ONE register open for people that either want a better/faster experience, or just want that good ole fashion human touch. In a recent bid to stop having patrons steal drinks - they've decided to remove the cups at the self-checkout area. They also don't have any pagers...

So you have to do the order - then go back in line and WAIT to get a cup.

And then they don't call your name, so you have to stand and wait - or constantly check at the counter for your food.

It's pretty clear that top to bottom things are falling apart overall. Modern commerce is an existential nightmare. The only companies I see weathering this right now are small and medium sized local businesses with a particular ideological bent. I see these major national services and large national and city infrastructure collapsing in less than 10 years. All of this isn't sustainable.

And ironically, virtually everyone (other than multi-nationals) is making less and less money.

Last month Google laid off 12,000+ employees supposedly... utter insanity given the nature of actually working with and for this company: https://finance.yahoo.com/news/google-lays-off-thousands-more-210822278.html

title: Youtube Stats & Information Every Digital Marketer Needs to Know
content:
• Table of Contents

    • Introduction
    • Understanding the Impact of YouTube Statistics on Marketing Strategies
    • How to Leverage YouTube User Demographics for Effective Marketing
    • The Role of YouTube in Digital Marketing: A Statistical Analysis
    • Exploring the Power of YouTube: Key Statistics for Marketers
    • Decoding YouTube Statistics: A Comprehensive Guide for Marketers
    • Conclusion

Introduction

This blog post from Olympia Marketing provides a comprehensive overview of key YouTube statistics that are crucial for marketers to understand. It offers insights into the platform's user base, viewing habits, and the effectiveness of YouTube as a marketing tool. The information is designed to help marketers leverage YouTube's potential in their digital marketing strategies.

Understanding the Impact of YouTube Statistics on Marketing Strategies

Understanding the impact of YouTube statistics on marketing strategies is crucial for any marketer aiming to leverage the power of this popular platform. As the second largest search engine in the world, YouTube has become a vital tool for businesses to reach their target audience and promote their products or services. The sheer volume of users and the diversity of content available make it a fertile ground for marketing efforts. To begin with, YouTube boasts over 2 billion logged-in monthly users. This staggering number represents a vast potential audience for marketers. The platform's global reach allows businesses to connect with consumers from all corners of the world, breaking down geographical barriers that traditional marketing methods might face. Moreover, YouTube's user demographics offer valuable insights for marketers. The platform is popular among all age groups, with 81% of 15-25 year-olds in the U.S. using YouTube. This wide age range provides opportunities for businesses to target different consumer segments, from tech-savvy millennials to older generations who are increasingly becoming comfortable with digital platforms. The viewing habits of YouTube users also play a significant role in shaping marketing strategies. On average, people spend 40 minutes per YouTube session, providing a substantial window for businesses to engage with their audience. Furthermore, mobile viewing makes up 70% of total watch time, highlighting the importance of optimizing content for mobile devices. YouTube's impact on consumer behavior is another key factor for marketers to consider. Studies show that 68% of YouTube users watched a video to help make a purchase decision. This statistic underscores the platform's influence on consumer buying habits and the potential for businesses to sway these decisions through effective video marketing. The type of content that resonates with YouTube users is also crucial in formulating a successful marketing strategy. How-to videos, product reviews, and tutorials are among the most popular types of content on the platform. Marketers can leverage this preference by creating informative and engaging videos that not only promote their products but also provide value to the viewer. The role of YouTube in driving website traffic should not be underestimated either. With 500 hours of video uploaded every minute, the platform offers endless opportunities for businesses to direct viewers to their websites. By including website links in video descriptions or using YouTube's card feature, marketers can effectively drive traffic and potentially increase conversions. Finally, the rise of YouTube influencers has opened up new avenues for marketing. Collaborations with influencers can help businesses reach a larger audience and enhance their credibility. With 70% of teenage YouTube subscribers trusting influencer opinions over traditional celebrities, influencer marketing on YouTube can be a powerful tool for businesses. In conclusion, understanding YouTube statistics is essential for marketers to effectively utilize the platform. The vast user base, diverse demographics, viewing habits, and content preferences all provide valuable data that can inform marketing strategies. By harnessing the power of YouTube, businesses can reach a global audience, influence consumer behavior, drive website traffic, and even leverage the influence of YouTube personalities. As the digital landscape continues to evolve, the importance of YouTube as a marketing tool is only set to increase.

How to Leverage YouTube User Demographics for Effective Marketing

As a marketer, understanding your audience is the cornerstone of any successful campaign. In the digital age, this means not only knowing who your audience is, but also where they are. With over 2 billion logged-in users per month, YouTube has become a vital platform for marketers to reach their target audience. Firstly, it's important to note that YouTube's user base is incredibly diverse. The platform is used by 73% of adults in the U.S., making it more popular than any other social media platform. This broad user base means that no matter who your target audience is, there's a good chance they're on YouTube. However, it's not enough to simply know that your audience is on the platform; you also need to understand how they use it. YouTube's user demographics reveal that the platform is most popular among 15-25 year olds, with this age group accounting for the largest proportion of users. However, it's also used by a significant number of older adults, with 38% of users aged 36-45 and 26% aged 56 and above. This means that while YouTube is a great platform for reaching younger audiences, it can also be effective for targeting older demographics. In terms of gender, YouTube's user base is fairly evenly split, with 50% of users identifying as female and 50% as male. This means that whether your target audience is predominantly male or female, YouTube is a viable platform for reaching them. Understanding how different demographics use YouTube can also help inform your marketing strategy. For example, research shows that users aged 18-24 are more likely to watch videos related to music or comedy, while those aged 35-44 are more likely to watch news or politics-related content. By understanding these preferences, you can tailor your content to appeal to your target audience. In addition to understanding YouTube's user demographics, it's also important to consider when your audience is most likely to be online. Research shows that YouTube traffic peaks in the evenings, particularly on weekends. By scheduling your content to go live during these peak times, you can increase the chances of your content being seen. Finally, it's worth noting that YouTube's user base is global, with the platform available in over 100 countries and 80 languages. This means that if your target audience is located in a specific country or speaks a specific language, you can tailor your content accordingly. In conclusion, understanding YouTube's user demographics can provide valuable insights that can help inform your marketing strategy. By knowing who your audience is, where they are, and how they use the platform, you can create content that resonates with them and ultimately drives engagement and conversions. Whether your target audience is young or old, male or female, based in the U.S. or overseas, YouTube offers a powerful platform for reaching them.

title: Wix & Problems With Their DNS/Domain System
content:
In the ever-evolving landscape of digital marketing and website development, businesses are continually striving to enhance their online presence. One such journey, involving a client's transition from a Wix-based website to a more sophisticated platform, unveils the intricacies of domain and DNS settings—a crucial, yet often under-appreciated, aspect of website management.

The Backstory - A Simple Update to a Client Site & Domain Settings Leads to Thousands in Extra Time on Dealing With Wix's DNS Idiosyncrasies

Our client, initially satisfied with the simplicity and cost-effectiveness of Wix, found themselves in need of a more robust website. They needed something a bit more sophisticated and with our unified goals for helping them market their business more - notably better search engine rankings, the integration of AdWords, and overall business growth online necessitated a platform with greater flexibility and capability.

This post is equal parts critique of Wix, which has indeed come a long way in improving its services, but still fails in many aspects - most notable - as we'll explain - their domain/DNS system.

The Challenge Unfolds

The transition seemed straightforward—until we encountered the domain and DNS configuration within Wix. Opting against transferring the domain to a third-party service like GoDaddy, which is our usual practice, we decided to adjust the DNS settings directly within Wix. This decision led us into a labyrinth of technical difficulties, the most prominent being the alarmingly slow DNS propagation.

DNS 101: A Quick Dive

Before delving further, let's touch upon the basics of DNS (Domain Name System). Essentially, DNS is the internet's way of converting human-friendly web addresses into computer-friendly IP addresses. This process involves several "hops" from the user's browser to various servers, translating domain names into the IP addresses where websites are hosted. Although this system is fundamentally simple, the journey through these hops can introduce numerous potential points of failure.

Domain & DNS Issues With Wix

In our venture, changing the DNS settings on Wix proved to be an ordeal. The expected quick and seamless update turned into a prolonged wait, significantly hindering the website's transition and accessibility. Despite the simplicity Wix offers for laymen setting up a website, we discovered that its DNS management system could severely impede progress in more complex scenarios.

For example - for more than a decade now it's been basically common knowledge that DNS changes happen immediately. Sure, 20 years ago - it could take 48 hours to propagate DNS changes, and although that's still a "best practice" its mostly anecdotal and no longer applies. In thousands of DNS changes in the last couple years alone, we haven't had a single case (UNTIL NOW!) where DNS changes took that long. In the case of this clients site - it took greater than 48 hours (UNHEARD OF) and STILL didn't fix the problem.

We ended up having to work with Wix's support multiple times - where they used some custom tools on their backend (unavailable to the public or to developers). Despite this, it still didn't solve the problems. It turns out that Wix, by default has some internal settings in terms of the way they handle subdomains (notably the "www" they like to use with each domain) that it's unable to address. This means it's virtually impossible to buy a domain through WIX right now - and setup an external website. So the only solution - after weeks of working with the client and WIX is transferring the domain COMPLETELY away from Wix.

Lessons Learned and the Path Forward

Our ordeal culminated in the decision to transfer the domain out of Wix and into GoDaddy, a move that, while resolving the issue, came at a significant cost. This experience serves as a cautionary tale for businesses and developers alike. It highlights the importance of understanding the intricacies of DNS management and the potential pitfalls of all-in-one web development platforms when undertaking more sophisticated web projects.

Conclusion

This journey through DNS dilemmas underscores a critical lesson: always plan for scalability and flexibility. As businesses grow, their online needs will evolve. Choosing a platform that can accommodate these changes without imposing undue technical and financial burdens is crucial. Let our story inform your decisions, helping you navigate the complex waters of website migration with greater foresight and preparedness.

Embrace the future with confidence, ensuring your digital infrastructure can adapt to your business's evolving needs. Reach out to us for expert guidance on making your online presence not just functional, but formidable.

title: Welcoming Nova Beauty Co. a Denton & Bartonville, TX Spa & Facial Facility
content:
Jane Ware has been a long time friend. Recently her and her husband decided to move to Denton Texas a quiet suburb just outside of the Dallas Fort Worth metro area. Within a few months she opened her spa and facial facility in Bartonville TX - Nova Beauty Co. and has been doing well in the local market ever since. She contacted us recently hoping she could step up her game as far as growing the business, attracting more clients from Bartonville and Denton looking for facials.

What We Did for Nova Beauty Co.

One thing many clients are surprised to learn when they work with Olympia Marketing - we don't have a set plan for every business. Because there are an infinite number of products, services, and markets, and we've had so much experience over the last 20 years, we prefer more of a "doctors" approach to working with clients...

It starts with simple questions like:

  • What's going on?
  • What are you looking to accomplish?
  • What (all) are you doing for marketing/advertising?
  • What does success look like?

We then take this information and apply our vast experience, as we develop a plan of action along with a proposal on costs and timelines and then execute swiftly.

In Nova Beauty's case the answers were similar to what we see with many businesses:

  • We need to grow. We must increase revenue by 2-3x and have the capacity for that from a personnel perspective immediately.
  • We'd like a clearer website, to do better in search, and ultimately achieve those revenue numbers.
  • They were not doing ANYTHING to market their business outside posting on social media (oh an unmanaged AdWords account the client didn't even remember until months-in to the engagement).
  • Success - don't want to spend anytime worrying about my website or marketing - just want to attend to clients - and for revenue and client counts to 2x-3x over the course of the year.

We did a thorough dive into the website, matched against these KPIs and their specific (modest budget).

Our Digital Plan for Nova Beauty

Our plan was as follows:

  • Optimize the website immediately, make it easier to use.
  • Solve some mobile problems/optimizations
  • The above would also allow us to achieve higher overall conversion rates for any ongoing SEO campaigns or advertising campaigns.
  • Take over adwords and optimize.
  • Create a modest initial SEO campaign.
  • Optimize existing Google My Business Page (including getting it verified) and make recommendations for ongoing updates to stay ahead of competitors.
  • Train their team on how to update their website to continue to dominate in local search.
  • Implement a blog so owner and staff could post ongoing and keep and further lead the conversation on spa and esthetician services in Bartonville.

In quickly learning about the Bartonville area we learned that besides being a small community outside Denton (itself a suburb of Dallas-Fort Worth Texas) we knew we could very quickly and easily optimize for certain key phrases that would lead to business. And by doing this and little else we'd be able to rank prominently and get relevant traffic to the website.

We then made some additional recommendations

Results for this Bartonville Spa & Esthetician

Within 1 month of execution (total time to execute was roughly 2 weeks start to finish):

  • Traffic up 70%+
  • Top 5 for Spa & Esthetician Services in Bartonville
  • Top 2 for Facials in Bartonville (the #1 phrase for people searching for this service)
  • AdWords - currently in the process of taking this over

Jane and company couldn't be happier and we look forward to further helping them scale and achieve all of their dreams.

title: The Danger of AI in Advertising
content:
How a client's "American Made" advertising highlights the dangers of AI and it's use in advertising (as well as the larger implications of this technology as it quietly spreads devious ideologies throughout society).

Something struck me today as I was working on a client account - two client accounts actually. Within AdWords - google has integrated it's AI system called "gemini" to help you create all kinds of assets while setting up accounts. This is a big help. Much of account setup and management is monotonous so having a tool to help create some of the small text and assets needed is definitely a game changer. It means people and companies like Olympia that manage Google AdWords PPC Accounts - can now concentrate on what matters most - analyzing the data and making changes that have big impacts - instead of having to spend hours on tiny text changes.

But, integration of these AI systems is very dangerous, even now - as I'll explain shortly.

Before We Get to the Details - let's take a step back and discuss some of the glaring issues with Artificial Intelligence.

Artificial Intelligence - an Ideological Nightmare

Google recently came under fire for some images that were created with it's AI in that it was refusing to create images of white people and supplanting white/europeans for blacks, and other darker-skinned people for peoples even when told NOT to and even when it IS NOT HISTORICALLY or FACTUALLY accurate.

Google has apologized for what it describes as “inaccuracies in some historical image generation depictions” with its Gemini AI tool, saying its attempts at creating a “wide range” of results missed the mark. The statement follows criticism that it depicted specific white figures (like the US Founding Fathers) or groups like Nazi-era German soldiers as people of color, possibly as an overcorrection to long-standing racial bias problems in AI.

What's ironic is that Google has been doing this for years - if you searched for various phrases, it was clear to a LOT of people that Google was trying to steer things ideologically and were providing results in a particular direction with a particular ideology. This is a VERY dangerous thing - especially for a TECH company to do be doing and I believe it's VERY important for people to understand how both their private tech data is being weaponized against them as well as how these tools are shaping reality through the manipulation of public perception (obviously this is a bit outside the scope of this blog), but we'll be successful at least in revealing how dangerous AI's implementation is weaponizing ideology and how it's already happening RIGHT NOW - TO YOU.

What is Ideology?

Ideology is a system of idea/ideals. It's the things you think, but can't remember why and those are bundled together with all kinds of assumptions you make about the world. Part of this is laziness, but part is time and sheer energy and brainpower. Not everyone can know everything as far as the collective understanding of the universe as we know it - and alternatively, if we knew everything, we'd be like God - so we rely on history, on the collective, on others - outsourcing much of what we believe or understand.

So at it's core ideology is all of your presuppositions (the things you just "believe" are true). Ironically, most don't realize how much belief is a part of life in the 21st century. We think all of those silly religious nuts and their beliefs were overthrown with logic and science. All of this stems from the enlightenment, as well as the changing culture that's been slowly moving towards where we are since at least the industrial revolution and certainly in the 20th century with the world wars.

But, IN FACT - BELIEF underscores, underpins, girdles EVERYTHING we say and do. The fastest way to understand this is just go back to being a 3 year old and ask "why?" To everything. It's annoying. But if you do this to yourself, you'll quickly realize... yes, in fact, things aren't what they seem - and you probably DON'T have good answers for things.

There seems to be a strange anomaly with man kind - whereby it takes us being immersed in language until we're children to start to begin to experience consciousness. Then as we get older, we start to realize and grow into all kinds of higher learning or higher levels of consciousness. It's only by looking back at our presuppositions (those things that were taught to us by friends, family, society - even during and through education) and asking questions (as we can now that we're "conscious") that we can begin to see the world more accurately.

One example of this is becoming aware of lying. How we can massage reality into whatever we want it to be - for others - by painting a different picture. When we're children we don't do this (although we do learn quickly), we have a voice in our head that tells us what we should/shouldn't do. As we begin to learn actions have consequences, and some of those consequences have bad feelings/punishments attached to them, we begin to learn to manipulate reality to prevent those feelings. And eventually a version of ourselves is born - often (these days in particular) we've ignored that inner voice for so long that it begins to completely disappear and we think we are the person we've been lying about this whole time.

Ironically, it's ALWAYS the children that are most affected by ideology. Why? Because they're not yet fully conscious enough to question their surroundings - to study history, or to reflect on their own experience and circumstances and apply it to what has been given to them as "right."

What is Nationalism?

What I'm about to present might sound COMPLETELY irrelevant - but it is entirely relevant (both for this story as well as for where we're currently at in culture and time... )

When I type "nationalism" - what do you think?

If you're like myself - you think/thought it's bad.

But, I realized about a decade ago that we in the West have a distorted view of nationalism. I started by asking myself "why?" Why is it "bad?" Well it was basically branding of a term and ideology - IE "nazis were nationalistic right? and nazis = bad right?" - so much of us collectively stopped understanding what it truly meant.

We outsourced our thinking to the collective and social order and the social order - through massive free-trade changes that have been happening since the 70s was saying... "nationalism bad..."

It wasn't until Trump's running for office in 2015 that I realized (as he was throwing that word around) that in, fact, nationalism wasn't what I thought it was. One hint at what it is, is found in the name itself (as so many keys to reality are found). The root comes from the word "natal" - or to be born. It is, at it's core - the love of the place one was born (the literal land) the culture, the people.

There is absolutely NOTHING wrong with this.

But, for some reason - as we all know - a particular group of people is not allowed to do this, to love itself (ESPECIALLY NOT COLLECTIVELY). And I'm not even advocating for that here - just pointing out patterns. I find it strange Google encourages businesses to mark all kinds of qualifications...

  • LGBTQ Owned/Friendly
  • Trans Friendly
  • Women Owned
  • Black Owned

But ONLY in a particular direction. Why not a "Christian Owned" business listing category?

Let's get back to how this ties into managing a local Google AdWords account and the danger of AI:

Artificial Intelligence Doesn't Like "American Made" Products

So it was with this background and mindset I'm sitting here this afternoon trying to optimize a clients' adwords account (two clients actually).

Client 1 - Veteran home services company, salt of the earth, worked hard for 40+ years and is trying to grow their company so they can provide a legacy for their children and grand children. Literally some of the best and kindest people I've ever known. Works hard to try to source all U.S. based manufacturers and parts for every job. All of their personnel are born and raised right here in Southwest Florida and that's important to them and all of their customers.

Client 2 - A real estate agent in the area - the TOP agent in the area actually. He was born here, raised here, and he KNOWS here - because it's literally a differentiator that makes him a great agent - knowledge of Southwest Florida that extends decades and an understanding of where things have been and a vision for the future.

In BOTH of the above cases - Google's gemini REFUSED to produce any content suggestions when I fed the above information into the tool to assist with suggestions.

I then ran some tests changing content and realized two things:

  • Any talk of being born or "from a location" is disallowed.
  • Similarly, any talk of "american-made" is being censored at the AI level.

Google clearly hates actually native born people (IE people born RIGHT NOW, in the place they're living, working and building their communities) this is independent of race/gender and it DEFINITELY hates the idea that even a small company would choose (at the will of its owner, AND ALL OF ITS customers...!) to choose American made parts and services. Absolutely absurd.

Where is this Heading?

I don't know where this is heading specifically, but it does raise a number of questions:

  • Now here's an interesting hypothetical - what happens when the language that underpins these ads is completely automated? It's clearly beginning to happen with advertising platforms today.
  • In any given day - what perspectives DON'T we see? Both from conventional human outlets, and now ESPECIALLY because things are being mediated through these ideologically outrageous AI tools?
  • Are ads already being throttled based on the companies behind them (Google's own viewpoint of the company from an ideological perspective)? - I know first hand that Google HAS gone after individuals and companies in the past for transgressions unrelated to their involvement with Google, completely illegally (and lost in court) - but in these cases one would never know and most wouldn't have the money to support a law suit even if it was revealed this is happening.
  • Is even the current nature of AI integration at this level already steering ads in a direction that's inline with Google's ideology (what even IS that ideology overall)?
  • Why are companies hell-bent on lecturing others on how to live? (reminds me of the failed "race" discussions Starbucks tried to push years ago.
  • How many people realize that the companies they support are actively trying to harm them through all kinds of subversive techniques?
  • Where does this end? We know that "climate change" is a massive narrative that's been spun up for nearly a hundred years (and been completely wrong time and again), but it's been thoroughly cemented in people's minds. Since people no longer have local communities to fight for (help grow, nourish, etc.) turning people's minds to more global "problems" has been an interesting move... but when does it end? We've traded the moral authority of religion, for the moral authority of the state and the ones with the largest companies and purses - this has to have been a step backwards (or a step closer to hell).
  • With all of our knowledge, and science, and technology - how could this have happened?

So What Do We Do?

I don't know. Unfortunately there's a whole giant economic system in place that basically forces small local businesses to use Google:

  • 90%+ of the browser market share
  • nearly 100% of the search share (both Google and #2 YouTube)

If you want to be found... it's only Google's services you MUST rely on for a host of businesses. I know many businesses that receive 90%+ of their total income directly from Google based clients (people searching and finding them on Google's services).

So the collective must begin to use alternatives. I know even though I have to use these tools for my work - I do what I can to minimize their use personally. I don't have a Google or Apple phone. I don't use Google for a majority of my searches.

But is this realistic in the aggregate? Not likely.

Policies could be enacted that shape this. Especially for a media company of this nature, guidelines could be put in place that disallow certain ideological insanities being forced on customers. Similarly much of AI seems to be free and open (from what I hear) behind the scenes - it's only the ideological public facing layer that prevents people from using it in certain ways. Policy could be enacted that disables this.

It's a strange time to be alive...
seo_title: AI in Advertising: Exploring the Dangers and Ethical Risks
seo_description: Explore the dangers of AI in advertising with Olympia Marketing. Understand the risks and implications for your business.

title: The Dark Side of Services Like UpWork
content:
Had a client recently ask about a very particular, niche project they have. They told me they were considering searching for someone through UpWork. I said that would be a good idea (I suspect they were hoping I could help with the project - but it was so obscure, although I personally could have helped, it would take me away from my clients and bread and butter and wouldn't be scalable so I wasn't interested in assisting).

But... I also warned the client of the dark side of services like upwork.

So what is the problem with upwork?

Doing a quick search for "upwork" i quickly found a ton of people that have been saying upwork sucks for sometime - and sadly this is the case.

What's the problem? The problem is hustle culture.

I've been trying to supplement our main in-house local team for some-time with contractors. I prefer U.S. based contractors for multiple reasons, not least of which is simply scheduling and communications.

But here's what I've experienced over the last 18 months of working with contractors from UpWork:

  • They virtually ALL suck - literally, they suck. They misrepresent their skills, claim they're better than they are, and wholly incapable of doing the job, thinking about the problem, etc.
  • The cost of actual capable designers, developers, and marketers, is getting exponentially more expensive - even with AI being a factor.
  • I've hired 4 different contractors to assist me with my load with Pay Per Click marketing - the first 2 literally lied, didn't do the work, missed deadlines, and flipped out when I refused payment. They were basically trying to "hustle" their way to a marketing agency - by pretending to do the work and outsourcing it (and their outsourced talent was doing the same thing!). A true house of cards. They left a scathing review because I refused payment - I have a feeling they paid their own contractors upfront lol.
  • In the few cases where I was able to find someone decent/capable, I've found people just don't want to work any more for money, no matter what the money is. In two cases, super simple ongoing updates with massive margins weren't "interesting enough" to warrant continued work.
  • Everyone Misrepresents themselves - be it their language abilities, coding skills, etc. I've found it valuable to quickly test them with a small project, for which we both have skin in the game, and move on if they fail any part of the test
  • No one is good at scheduling, communication, or business (in my experience) on UpWork.

You have men like Andrew Tate (who all things aside, does seem to be a capable business man - or at least have people behind him that completely are) advocating for all kinds of "hustling." Business is hard, you have to bring value, be consitent, communicate, etc. Service businesses are MUCH harder than product businesses because you're selling your time and expertise. In hindsight it's no wonder that we're in this mess.

We're at the start of the competancy crisis and things are going to get MUCH worse here on out. Imagine this... in the HVAC industry - for example - right now for every 7 techs retiring only 1 is entering the field. Apply this to electricity, engineering, the legal field and more... things are going to be collapsing quickly in my opinion over the next 15 years.
seo_title: UpWork's Dark Side: Protect Your Freelance Earnings
seo_description: Freelancing on UpWork? Olympia Marketing exposes the downsides. Learn how to navigate challenges and safeguard your freelance career.

title: SEO Meaning in Digital Marketing
content:
All industries have their jargon. In the digital marketing realm we have all kinds of jargon and acronyms...

PPC, CTR, IP, CPM, MMR, etc. etc. etc. - it's almost never ending. But, one of the more important words

With everything you can do for your business as far as marketing and in digital marketing in particular, Search Engine Optimization (SEO) stands out as a fundamental strategy that can make or break an online presence. Before we get to some specifics on SEO, and my typical recommendations let's discuss the basics. This blog post delves into the meaning of SEO in the context of digital marketing, exploring its significance, key components, and practical tips to enhance your visibility and reach online.

What is SEO?

SEO, which stands for Search Engine Optimization, refers to the process of optimizing your website and its content to rank higher in search engine results pages (SERPs). These days they should really just call it "GO" (google optimization) as so much search traffic and web traffic overall is dominated by the Google search engine and the Chrome browser.

The goal of SEO is to increase the quantity and quality of traffic to your website through organic search engine results, which are unpaid. You're effectively doing what it takes to:

  • Get Google To Trust You
  • Get Google To Rank Your Website Higher
  • Get Google to Trust/Rank Your Website Higher Than Competitors

SEO is crucial because it makes your website more visible, which means more traffic and more opportunities to convert prospects into customers.

Here's an example:

I did a search for "Estero Marketing Company" - and Olympia Marketing appears #7 in the results. The trick with SEO is ... asking yourself a couple questions:

  • Is that particular search relevant to your audience?
  • Are your customers searching for that?
  • If they ARE searching for that and find you... will they "convert" (email you, fill out a form, or pick up the phone)?

If all of the above is answered in the affirmative then a couple other questions come into play (which we'll answer below):

  • Why are you only #7 in placement overall?
  • What can we do to increase this placement?

In some ways you can think of SEO as the yellow pages, but instead of just titling your company AAA Plumbing to come up first (or paying for an ad) - you actually need to do some technical WORK - as Google rewards websites and companies based on how helpful they are.

Key Components of SEO

At the CORE of SEO I like to talk to clients, potential clients, and those I'm educating about the field that it's basically TWO major things...

  • Your Website: What is actually happening on the website, including your content, keywords, frequency of updates, code, etc.
  • Off Site "Stuff": What on the web is out there about your company that will "look good" in Google's eyes - this includes other websites mentioning you, news mentions, PR, links in to your website. The list is actually pretty small.

By knowing the above and combining that with what we know about Google (they're not explicit about what they're looking for - so SEO is as much art as it is a science) we can increase the things that look good, minimize the things that look bad to Google and grow your website in ranking - to help you achieve traffic. Let's look at some additional specifics that everyone always likes to cover:

Keywords: At the heart of SEO are keywords. These are the terms and phrases that users enter into search engines. Understanding the right keywords to target, based on your audience's needs and search behaviors, is essential for effective SEO.

Content: Content is the vehicle you use to reach and engage audiences. Not only does it need to be high-quality, informative, and relevant, but it also needs to be optimized for the keywords you’re targeting.

On-Page SEO: This involves optimizing individual web pages in order to rank higher and earn morerelevant traffic. On-page refers to both the content and HTML source code of a page that can be optimized, as opposed to off-page SEO which refers to links and other external signals.

Off-Page SEO: It focuses on enhancing the authority of your domain through the act of getting links from other websites. A good volume of high-quality backlinks tells search engines that your website is valuable and worth ranking well.

Technical SEO: This aspect of SEO focuses on improving the technical elements of your website to increase the ranking of its pages in the search engines. This includes improving the site’s loading speed, ensuring it is mobile-friendly, securing it with HTTPS, and enhancing usability.

Why is SEO Important in Digital Marketing?

Although SEO is important, and powerful - I NEVER start projects off worried too much about SEO. Yes, SEO is one of the most powerful forms of marketing, but in our modern "I want it now culture" virtually no clients are ready to understand that SEO is generally a multi-year process (particularly if you have a new website or a new business). I did have a recent scenario however, where - because of a brand new market in the AI space, SEO was able to absolutely EXPLODE a business for virtually nothing, albeit once the owners had gotten a taste for the sweet sweet SEO traffic and huge revenues, they ended up strategically investing in SEO ongoing.

SEO is one of the most cost-effective marketing strategies because it targets users who are actively looking for your products and services online. The inbound nature of SEO helps businesses save money as opposed to outbound strategies like cold-calling. By targeting users searching for terms related to your industry, SEO is more qualified than many other marketing strategies, resulting in cost-savings for companies.

Practical Tips for Implementing Effective SEO Strategies

Audit your website: Understanding the current state of your website is the first step in implementing effective SEO strategies. Use tools like Google Analytics and Google Search Console to identify areas of improvement.

Optimize for mobile: With increasing mobile usage, mobile optimization is essential for SEO. Ensure your website is responsive and provides a good user experience on mobile devices.

Focus on user experience (UX): A site with a great user experience is more likely to keep visitors on your site longer and convert them into customers. Make sure your website is easy to navigate, has a fast load time, and contains valuable content.

Build quality backlinks: Earning backlinks from reputable and relevant sites can significantly boost your SEO ranking. Focus on creating great content that others want to link to and engage in guest blogging on respected sites in your industry.

The role of SEO in digital marketing is indispensable as it is a powerful tool to build visibility and credibility online. By understanding the meaning of SEO and implementing robust strategies, businesses can significantly improve their online presence and increase their chances of success in the digital world. Remember, SEO is a continuous process that requires patience and persistence, but the long-term benefits are well worth the effort.
seo_title: SEO Meaning: Digital Marketing Strategies for Online Success
seo_description: SEO meaning: Olympia Marketing explains SEO's importance in digital marketing. Improve your online presence with effective SEO strategies.
